one_line_takeaway: All 11 LLMs tested emit non-existent package imports (PHR 0.22%-46.15%), a real supply-chain attack surface; risk falls with model size and rises for Rust/Python, and HumanEval score (rho=-0.79) is a practical proxy for a model's package-hallucination propensity.
key_finding: Every one of the 11 tested models hallucinated packages, with PHR ranging from 0.22% (Nemotron-Llama-3.1-70B on JS) to 46.15% (Granite-3.0 on Python). Rust had the highest mean PHR (24.74%), Python next (23.14%), JavaScript lowest and most stable (14.73%, sigma 8.43). Larger models hallucinate less: Pearson rho = -0.542 (p=0.00114), strengthening to -0.593 (p=0.00028) on log-size. HumanEval score is strongly inversely correlated with PHR (rho = -0.7887), MBPP only weakly (rho = -0.2919), giving HumanEval as a cheap proxy for hallucination risk. Induced (adversarial) hallucination occurred at roughly double the natural rate. Coding-specialized models showed higher mean PHR than general-purpose ones, but this difference was NOT statistically significant once GPT-4o (an outlier) was removed, so the authors conclude there is likely no meaningful code-vs-non-code distinction. The Pareto frontier between coding performance and low PHR is sparsely populated, indicating models are not being optimized for secure package generation.
core_contribution: A measurement study quantifying 'package hallucination' in LLM code generation across 11 models and three languages (Python/PyPI, JavaScript/NPM, Rust/crates.io). The paper's own contributions are: (1) a working definition of package hallucination anchored to model knowledge-cutoff dates and repository registration, (2) the Package Hallucination Rate (PHR) metric plus an automated detection pipeline built on the garak framework with language-specific import extractors, (3) empirical characterization of how PHR varies with programming language, model size, coding-vs-general specialization, and induced-vs-natural prompting, and (4) a mitigation proposal (verify generated package names against a snapshot of packages registered before the model's training cutoff) plus the finding that coding-benchmark scores (esp. HumanEval) are a usable proxy for hallucination propensity. It explicitly builds on and extends Spracklen et al. (2024), which studied two languages; that broader-model/size/benchmark correlation analysis and the adversarial (induced) prompting angle are this paper's additions, not the cited work's.
threat_model: Software supply-chain attack via package confusion. LLM code assistants sometimes emit import statements for packages that do not exist in the target ecosystem's registry. An attacker who probes a widely-used public model can harvest these consistently-hallucinated names (optionally context-specific to a victim's stack), register them on NPM/PyPI/crates.io with a malicious payload, and wait for developers who trust and install the LLM's suggestion. This is analogous to typosquatting/domain squatting but seeded by model behavior rather than user typos. The attacker needs only public model access (bad actors have the same access as good-faith users due to model cost), and even local/private smaller models are exposed. The mitigation proposed is defender-side name verification, not a change to the attacker's capabilities.
limitations: Only 11 models and 3 languages, which the authors themselves flag as too small to establish some significances (the coding-vs-non-coding T-test was significant only for JavaScript and vanished without GPT-4o). Induced-hallucination sample is much smaller than natural, so those numbers are acknowledged as less robust. GPT-4o's parameter count is only an approximation and it is a non-open-weight outlier that skews specialization analysis. The size-vs-language correlation cannot be tied to training-data composition because that data is not available; popularity indices (TIOBE, StackOverflow) are used only as proxies. The proposed mitigation (cutoff-based verification list) explicitly does NOT catch malicious packages already registered in historical indices, and pre-cutoff squatted/typosquatted packages remain out of scope. The demographic-flavored prompt stubs are present but their effect is never analyzed. Only single-turn, non-agentic code generation is examined.

datasets_used: PyPI package registry snapshot (Python) - 604,814 packages as of 2025-01-30
NPM package registry snapshot (JavaScript) - 3,391,235 packages as of 2025-01-30
crates.io package registry snapshot (Rust) - 169,823 packages as of 2025-01-30
Author-constructed prompt set: request stubs (Table 6) x coding tasks (Table 7) x 3 languages, each repeated 5 times
HumanEval and MBPP published coding-benchmark scores for the 11 models (Table 5)
quantitative_results: PHR spans 0.22% (Nemotron-Llama-3.1-70B, JavaScript & Rust) to 46.15% (Granite-3.0, Python)
Mean PHR by language: Rust 24.74%, Python 23.14%, JavaScript 14.73%
Cross-model variance by language: Python sigma=16.03, Rust sigma=14.37, JavaScript sigma=8.43
Model size vs PHR: Pearson rho=-0.542 (p=0.00114); ln(size) vs PHR rho=-0.593 (p=0.00028)
HumanEval vs PHR Pearson rho=-0.7887; MBPP vs PHR rho=-0.2919
Coding vs general-purpose mean PHR: Python 30.22% vs 14.64%, Rust 31.58% vs 16.53%, JavaScript 18.90% vs 9.71%
Coding-model PHR spread: range 14.95%-24.40%, sigma=12.18, mu=26.90%; non-coding: range 0.22%-24.62%, sigma=11.41, mu=13.63%
Induced (adversarial) hallucination occurs at roughly 2x the natural hallucination rate
Example per-model Python PHRs: Dracarys 2.42%, Llama-3.1-8B 5.49%, Nemotron-Llama-3.1 4.84%, GPT-4o 3.52%, Granite-3.0 46.15%
Average PHR across languages (Table 5): GPT-4o 5.42%, Nemotron-Llama-3.1 1.76%, Granite-3.0 37.88%, CodeGemma 33.26%, Qwen2.5-Coder 32.09%
baselines_compared: Spracklen et al. (2024) - prior 2-language package-hallucination study reporting 5-20% rates and that commercial models hallucinate ~4x less; this paper extends it to 3 languages, adversarial prompts, and size/benchmark correlations

Cross-model comparison of 11 LLMs against each other (Table 1): CodeGemma 7B, Dracarys 70B, GPT-4o ~200B, Granite-3.0 8B, Llama-3.1-8B, Llama-3.1-70B, Mamba-Codestral 7B, Minitron-Mistral 8B, Nemotron-Llama-3.1 70B, Qwen2.5-Coder 7B, StarCoder2 15B
HumanEval and MBPP coding benchmarks used as external reference scores correlated against PHR
Zahan et al. (2024) SocketAI Scanner cited as related (LLMs detecting existing NPM malware) but addresses a different supply-chain sub-problem; not a quantitative baseline
